title: Hierarchical historical pit-window quantile priors
summary: Official F1 glossary defines a pit window from tyre life data, and recent tyre-degradation work emphasizes degradation as a key driver of stop timing.
rationale: Official F1 glossary defines a pit window from tyre life data, and recent tyre-degradation work emphasizes degradation as a key driver of stop timing. The current best code uses fixed global service/finish ages; a stronger prior is likely the empirical stop-age distribution conditioned on regime, because Monaco/Monza/Qatar-style tracks and wet compounds distort global tyre-life assumptions.
implementation_hint: From training only and in chronological OOF fashion, estimate smoothed stop-age quantiles and stop-progress quantiles for groups such as (wet/dry, Compound), (Race, Compound), (Race, Compound, Stint), and (Year, Race, Compound). Add features like distance to P25/P50/P75 stop age, percentile rank of current TyreLife within historical stop ages, and residual to expected laps-remaining-at-stop. Use hierarchical backoff when counts are low.
expected_effect: Should replace brittle hand-picked tyre-life constants with data-driven, race-specific pit-window pressure while staying compact enough for tree models.
risk: If not built with expanding folds, these priors leak future races/laps. Sparse groups also need strong smoothing or they will overfit rare drivers/races.
